did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used artificial intelligence models, specifically comparing standard machine learning methods (XGBoost, k-NN, MLP, SVM) and a 1D Convolutional Neural Network (1D-CNN), to classify Raman spectral data for the identification and differentiation of nonmelanoma skin cancer structures (basal cell carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma) in ex vivo skin tissues.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'RESEARCH PAPER' in the header. It presents original experimental results from a study involving 332 nonmelanoma skin cancer specimens, describing the development and validation of an AI model for spectral classification, which constitutes new empirical findings rather than a summary of prior literature.

title: AI-assisted identification of nonmelanoma skin cancer structures based on combined line-field confocal optical coherence tomography and confocal Raman microspectroscopy
brief_summary: This study developed a compact, noninvasive system combining line-field confocal optical coherence tomography (LC-OCT) and confocal Raman microspectroscopy to identify nonmelanoma skin cancer structures in ex vivo tissues. The researchers imaged more than 330 specimens from 332 patients, acquiring over 1300 spectral acquisitions to analyze basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and squamous cell carcinoma (SCC). To classify these structures, they trained artificial intelligence models, comparing standard machine learning methods (XGBoost, k-NN, MLP, SVM) with a 1D Convolutional Neural Network (1D-CNN). The evaluation utilized a fivefold cross-validation scheme, with the 1D-CNN model demonstrating the highest performance. The AI model achieved an area under the ROC curve of 0.95 for BCC structures and 0.92 when including both BCC and SCC structures. The results indicate that spectral attention scores can reveal key chemical differences among cancerous structures, supporting accurate morpho-chemical characterization.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target of the study; 247 specimens analyzed. AI models differentiated BCC structures (LOB-T, EPI-T) from healthy tissues (EPI-H, DER-H). AUC of 0.95 for BCC structures.
Secondary target of the study; 85 specimens analyzed. Included in a multiclass model with BCC targets. AUC of 0.92 for combined BCC and SCC structures.

paper_terms: epidermis in the in sano zone
lesion_types_studied.details: Healthy control tissue from surgical margins used for comparison against tumor tissues in both BCC and SCC datasets.

paper_terms: dermis in the in sano zone
lesion_types_studied.details: Healthy control tissue from surgical margins used for comparison against tumor tissues in the BCC dataset.
Mentioned in the Introduction as a category of skin cancer (10% of skin cancers) for context, but not studied or included in the experiments.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of nonmelanoma skin cancer structures (Basal Cell Carcinoma lobules, Squamous Cell Carcinoma tumor, healthy epidermis, healthy dermis).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the AI models were trained to 'classify' Raman spectral data. Two multiclass classification models were developed: one with four classes for BCC targets (EPI-H, EPI-T, DER-H, LOB-T) and another with five classes including SCC targets (EPI-H, EPI-T, DER-H, LOB-T, TUM-T). Performance was evaluated using Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) and confusion matrices for class prediction.

datasets_used.name: Saint-Etienne University Hospital Ex Vivo Skin Specimen Dataset
datasets_used.description: A proprietary dataset of fresh, unprocessed ex vivo surgical skin specimens collected from the dermatology department of Saint-Etienne University Hospital, France, over a 1-year period. The specimens were histopathologically confirmed and imaged using a combined LC-OCT and confocal Raman microspectroscopy system.
classes_used: Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) targets: Epidermis above BCC lobules (EPI-T), BCC lobules (LOB-T), Healthy epidermis (EPI-H), Healthy dermis (DER-H)
Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) targets: Tumor (TUM-T), Healthy epidermis (EPI-H)

use.training: Used to train and validate artificial intelligence models (XGBoost, k-NN, MLP, SVM, and 1D-CNN) via a fivefold cross-validation scheme stratified by labels with patient grouping to prevent data leakage.
use.validation: Used for validation within the fivefold cross-validation scheme.
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation within the fivefold cross-validation scheme.
use.other: The dataset was acquired from 332 patients (247 BCC lesions, 85 SCC lesions). The total number of spectra analyzed was 1382.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of AI models (XGBoost, k-NN, MLP, SVM, 1D-CNN) and prevent data leakage by stratifying by labels and grouping by patient.
To quantify classification performance for binary 'one-vs-rest' tasks and multiclass setups (averaged class-wise AUC).
To display prediction performance, showing raw counts and proportions of samples predicted as each class among all samples with the given ground truth class.
To quantitatively assess the consistency of spectral attention score vectors across different cross-validation folds for each target class.
To normalize absolute variations in spectral attention scores to a 0 to 1 scale.
Applied for deconvolution of Raman spectra to minimize the reduced chi-square value.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, significance tests) was reported in the paper. The study evaluated model performance using Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC), confusion matrices, recall, sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, and precision, along with spectral attention scores and pair-wise cosine similarity for consistency assessment, but did not perform statistical hypothesis testing to compare groups or validate significance.

Saint-Etienne University Hospital Ex Viv.experiment: BCC and SCC Targets Classification (5 classes: EPI-H, EPI-T, DER-H, LOB-T, TUM-T)
Saint-Etienne University Hospital Ex Viv.notes: Performance for BCC lobules and DER-H identification was marginally affected compared to the BCC-only model. EPI-H targets from SCC were merged with EPI-H targets from BCC. TUM-T and EPI-T showed high confusion with EPI-H due to biochemical similarity (keratins/proteins).

architecture_details.heads: Single classification head with 5 output features (for BCC and SCC targets model); 4 output features (for BCC targets model)
architecture_details.classifier_head: Fully connected layers ending in softmax/sigmoid for multiclass classification
architecture_details.training_schedule: Fivefold cross-validation, stratified by labels, with patients grouped to prevent data leakage

training_details.data_splitting: Stratified by labels, grouped by patient
training_details.classes: 4 classes (BCC targets: EPI-H, EPI-T, DER-H, LOB-T) or 5 classes (BCC and SCC targets: EPI-H, EPI-T, DER-H, LOB-T, TUM-T)

classification_or_output_head.outputs: 5 classes for combined BCC/SCC model; 4 classes for BCC-only model
classification_or_output_head.attention_mechanism: Occlusion method with sliding window size 13 and stride 5 to generate spectral attention scores

preprocessing_techniques: Division of spectra into three spectral ranges (800 to 1350, 1350 to 1750, and 2800 to 3000 cm−1)
Extension of the range by 50 cm−1 at both ends to avoid edge effects
Baseline correction using polynomial fitting (order 5 for first two ranges, order 1 for last range) with asymmetric quadratic function
Removal of the extended margins added in the extension step
Application of Savitzky–Golay filter (second order, window size 13 points)
Concatenation of preprocessed data from the three ranges to form final input data
conclusions: The developed AI model, specifically the 1D-CNN, demonstrated high classification performance for identifying nonmelanoma skin cancer structures, achieving an area under the ROC curve (AUC) of 0.95 for basal cell carcinoma (BCC) structures and 0.92 when including both BCC and squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) structures.

Spectral attention scores derived from Raman data revealed key chemical differences among various cancerous structures, with high attention scores aligning with known specific biochemical characteristics such as protein and lipid content.

The system proved capable of discriminating BCC structures even for those with similar morphological features, leveraging the complementary information from combined line-field confocal optical coherence tomography (LC-OCT) and confocal Raman microspectroscopy.

While encouraging results were obtained for SCC, the differentiation of epidermal structures in SCC currently lacked accuracy compared to BCC, potentially due to subtle biochemical changes or the presence of cancerization in areas labeled as healthy.
Future investigations could utilize the system's chemical differentiation capabilities for the chemical subtyping of BCC to guide therapy options or to identify the nature of unusual structures.
Future technical developments should focus on facilitating in vivo use of the system, particularly by reducing the acquisition time of Raman spectra.

Alternative approaches such as semi-supervised learning, which combines annotated spectra with unlabeled data, could be explored in future work to improve accuracy given the current limitation of annotated spectral data.
important_remarks: The study is limited to ex vivo fresh surgical specimens; the device's current Raman acquisition time of 1 minute makes in vivo clinical deployment difficult.
Data leakage was mitigated by grouping spectra by patient during fivefold cross-validation, ensuring no individual appeared in both training and validation sets.
The dataset is single-center (Saint-Etienne University Hospital, France), limiting external generalizability.

Differentiation of epidermal structures in Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) lacked accuracy compared to Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), potentially due to subtle biochemical changes or cancerization in areas labeled as healthy.
The number of annotated spectra is limited, suggesting future work should explore semi-supervised learning to leverage unlabeled data.
Some 'healthy' epidermis (EPI-H) spectra may have been part of the cancerization process, leading to potential labeling inaccuracies and confusion with tumor epidermis (EPI-T).
High confusion was observed between TUM-T (SCC tumor) and EPI-T (epidermis above BCC lobules) due to biochemical similarities (keratins/proteins).
The study did not perform formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values); performance was evaluated using AUC, confusion matrices, and spectral attention scores.
